questions.question: What is the main goal of tracking progress in a system design prep repo?
options: To prove that every lesson was read line by line regardless of learning outcome.
To preserve enough evidence that you can choose the next lesson based on actual gaps and momentum.
To replace mock interviews with automated completion counts.
To avoid keeping any notes outside a single README file.
questions.explanation: Progress tracking should drive better next decisions, not create vanity metrics.

questions.question: Which metadata is most important to keep friction low while still preserving learning signal?
options: A compact status, timestamp, and pointers to notes or artifacts.
A detailed paragraph after every study session whether useful or not.
A full architecture diagram embedded in the progress file.
Every intermediate thought captured as a revision history entry.
questions.explanation: Minimal required metadata is more likely to be updated consistently.

questions.question: Why is a local machine-readable progress file useful in this course?
options: It makes it easier to validate state, recommend next work, and keep lesson status separate from lesson content.
It guarantees all lesson scores will improve automatically over time.
It allows the learner to skip debriefs because the file is authoritative.
It is required because markdown cannot represent course state.
questions.explanation: A small structured file helps separate curriculum structure from current learner state.

questions.question: Which failure is most dangerous for a progress tracker?
options: The file uses JSON instead of YAML.
The tracker shows many lessons done, but notes, quiz evidence, and follow-up signals are missing or stale.
Artifact paths are kept near lesson folders.
The roadmap contains more than twenty phases.
questions.explanation: False confidence is worse than having no tracker because it hides missing learning evidence.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping the progress data model deliberately small?
options: It improves consistency and maintenance cost, but limits rich analytics unless you derive them later.
It increases storage cost, but reduces bandwidth cost.
It removes the need to track mock interviews entirely.
It makes assisted lessons impossible to record honestly.
questions.explanation: A small schema keeps updates cheap, but richer reporting may need secondary processing.

questions.question: Which observability signal best indicates that the prep workflow itself is healthy?
options: The number of subdirectories in the repository.
How quickly the progress state is updated after sessions and whether notes or artifacts are linked consistently.
The number of times the learner opens the roadmap file.
The total size of markdown files committed each week.
questions.explanation: Fresh, linked evidence shows the workflow is actually being used rather than merely existing.

questions.question: If two learners share the same repo for pair practice, what design concern becomes more important?
options: Separating learner-specific progress state from shared lesson content and artifacts.
Removing timestamps so no one overwrites them accidentally.
Storing architecture diagrams inside the Go binaries.
Disabling validation to keep collaboration flexible.
questions.explanation: Shared content and individual state should not be conflated when multiple learners collaborate.

questions.question: Why should this workflow distinguish `assisted` from `done`?
options: Because assisted attempts still reflect progress, but they signal a different confidence level and may deserve retesting later.
Because only unassisted lessons may be kept in the repository.
Because interviewers will ask for the raw status values during interviews.
Because lessons with quizzes cannot be completed without assistance.
questions.explanation: The distinction preserves honesty without treating help as failure.
